Max K guaranteed coverable by 43 north/south canals in a 2025×2025 grid

In a 2025×2025 grid, some cells are marked (contain seedlings). You may build canals of two types:

- Northbound canals: lattice paths from (1,1) to (2025,2025) using only East (E) and North (N) steps.

- Southbound canals: lattice paths from (1,2025) to (2025,1) using only East (E) and South (S) steps.

A canal "covers" a marked cell if it passes through that cell. You may choose the canals after seeing the marked set.

You are allowed to build at most 43 canals total (each canal may be of either type).

Find the largest integer K such that, no matter which K cells are marked, it is always possible to choose 43 canals that cover all marked cells.

Solution.
Step 1 (poset_modeling; cover_chains_with_northbound; cover_antichains_with_southbound).
Model the grid cells as elements of the product poset [2025]×[2025] with order
  (x1,y1) \leq (x2,y2)  ⇔  x1\leqx2 and y1\leqy2.

- A northbound canal visits cells in nondecreasing x and nondecreasing y, hence its visited set is a chain in this poset.
Conversely, any finite chain can be visited by some northbound canal: list the chain points in increasing order and route an E/N path through them.

- If A is an antichain in this poset, then when its points are listed by increasing x, the y-coordinates strictly decrease (otherwise two would be comparable).
Thus a southbound canal (E/S) can be routed to visit all points of A in that x-order (moving E to increase x and S to decrease y).

So: northbound canals cover chains; southbound canals cover antichains.

Step 2 (sufficiency_via_greedy_and_mirsky).
Claim: Any set P of 989 marked cells can be covered by 43 canals.

Let P0=P. For i\geq0, let hi be the maximum size of a chain in Pi (the "height"), and remove from Pi a chain of size hi to obtain Pi+1.
Thus
  h0 \geq h1 \geq h2 \geq ...,
and after removing i chains, we have used i northbound canals.

By Mirsky's theorem (dual of Dilworth), Pi can be partitioned into exactly hi antichains, hence covered by hi southbound canals.
Therefore, for any i,
  Pi can be covered by (i northbound canals) + (hi southbound canals) = i+hi canals.

So if there exists i with i+hi \leq 43, then P is covered by 43 canals.

Assume for contradiction that no such i exists, i.e.
  i+hi \geq 44  for all i = 0,1,...,43.
Then
  h0 \geq 44,
  h1 \geq 43,
  ...
  h42 \geq 2,
  h43 \geq 1.
In particular, after removing 43 chains, P43 is still nonempty (since h43\geq1), so at least 1 marked cell remains.

But the total number of removed cells is at least
  h0+h1+...+h42  \geq 44+43+...+2,
and including the remaining \geq1 cell gives
  |P| = |P0| \geq (44+43+...+2) + 1 = (1+2+...+44) = 44 \cdot 45/2 = 990,
contradicting |P|=989.

Hence some i satisfies i+hi \leq 43, and 43 canals always suffice to cover any 989 marked cells.

Step 3 (necessity_via_block_construction).
Claim: There exists a set Q of 990 marked cells that cannot be covered by 43 canals.

Construct 44 "blocks" B1,...,B44 as follows. Define
  a_j = 1 + (j-1)j/2   (j=1,...,44),
and let
  B_j = { (a_j + t, a_j + (j-1-t)) : t=0,1,...,j-1 }.
Each Bj has exactly j points.

(i) Each Bj is an antichain:
If t1<t2 then x increases (a_j+t1 < a_j+t2) while y decreases (a_j+(j-1-t1) > a_j+(j-1-t2)), so the two points are incomparable.

Thus any northbound canal (a chain) meets Bj in at most one point.

(ii) A southbound canal meets at most one block:
Each Bj lies inside the axis-aligned square
  R_j = [a_j, a_j+j-1] × [a_j, a_j+j-1].
Note that the top-right corner coordinate is
  a_j+j-1 = 1 + (j-1)j/2 + j-1 = j(j+1)/2,
while the next block begins at
  a_{j+1} = 1 + j(j+1)/2.
Hence for i<j we have
  max x in R_i < min x in R_j   and   max y in R_i < min y in R_j.
So R_j is strictly above and to the right of R_i.

A southbound canal moves only E (x increases) and S (y decreases). Therefore it cannot intersect two such rectangles:
- it cannot go from R_i to R_j with i<j because that would require y to increase (since all points of R_j have y > max y of R_i),
- and it cannot go from R_j to R_i because that would require x to decrease.

Thus any southbound canal intersects at most one Bj.

Now let a be the number of northbound canals among any chosen 43 canals; then the number of southbound canals is 43-a.

Because each Bj is an antichain, a northbound canal covers at most 1 point of Bj, so a northbound canals cover at most a points of Bj.
Therefore, for every block Bj with j>a, at least one point of Bj is not covered by the northbound canals, so Bj must be intersected by at least one southbound canal.

But the blocks with j>a are exactly
  B_{a+1}, B_{a+2}, ..., B_{44},
which is 44-a blocks, each requiring its own southbound canal (since a southbound canal hits at most one block).
Hence we need at least 44-a southbound canals.

This is impossible because we only have 43-a southbound canals:
  43-a < 44-a.

Therefore Q := B1 ∪ ... ∪ B44 (which has size 1+2+...+44 = 990) cannot be covered by 43 canals.

Step 4 (compute_maximum_K).
From Step 2, every set of 989 marked cells is always coverable by 43 canals.
From Step 3, there exists a set of 990 marked cells not coverable by 43 canals.

Therefore the largest guaranteed K is
  K = 989.

Uniqueness Test
1) (Upper bound.) Step 2 proves that 43 canals suffice for every marked set of size 989, so the guaranteed maximum K is at least 989.

2) (Lower bound.) Step 3 constructs an explicit marked set of size 990 that 43 canals cannot cover, so the guaranteed maximum K is at most 989.

3) Since both bounds match, the maximal guaranteed K is uniquely determined and equals 989.

989
